speaker: interviewer
text: Can't you pull back? If you could just turn it around and actually read it to yourself, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December the 3rd, 1995. Santa Erder Pelham is being interviewed by Sybil Castle in Capetown, South Africa. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you on yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Right. Morning, Santa. Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Santa Pelham, S-A-N-T-A. The surname is Pelham, P-E-L-H-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Erder, E-R-D-E-R. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No nicknames.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your birth date, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 26th of March, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And what is your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And your-- the city of your birth, or the town where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Knapsack, which is about 10 or 12 kilometers from Koln, Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Rhine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Knapsack, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. K-N-A-P-S-A-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And the country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany. Thank you. I'd like you now to describe your li-- what your life was like before the Holocaust, before the occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my parents were born in Poland. So they were Polish citizens. They married in 1913. And when in 1914, the war started, the Germans invaded Poland, they came to Warsaw. And they were very benign. There were no Nazis at that time. And they urged, especially Jews somehow, they urged Jews to come to the Rhineland, which was very industrialized. They needed workers to relieve the German soldiers, the German-- German men to go into the field. Because in the end, the war start-- lasted, after all, four years, and they needed these soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they needed these men. And my father was offered to go-- to come to the Rhineland. And in 1916, I think it was, he came to Germany. And my mother followed very soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they-- I-- I was born in 1918. But they were-- they had somebody staying with them, a friend, a young man, whose name was Simon, Simon Puterflam. He also came from Poland, and he was about 18 at that time. And my parents at that time were, what, 24, 25, something like this. I don't know exactly. And they became very warm friends. In-- when I was on my way, my-- my-- my mother had her-- had her pangs, her-- her birth-- she wanted to give birth to me-- my father was not available because he was working in the factories for the Germans. So she sent this young man for the midwife. And he ran, brought the midwife, and I was born in 19-- in-- on the 26th of March. That was just a few months before the war ended. So this young man knew me. And I was, most probably, a lovely little baby. And he-- when the war ended in November, 1918, he said goodbye to my parents. He thanked them very much. They were very good to him. They were like family. But he had made arrangement with his brother that after the war, they would meet in Berlin. My parents heard nothing of him since then until much, much later. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood. I-- I-- we had lots of warmth in-- in-- in our home, lots and lots of warmth. We were-- we children-- well, my brothers were born. I had my eldest bro-- my elder brother-- is it elder or eldest?

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was born in 1921. And soon after, 11 months after, my other brother was born. His name was Chaim. And all I remember of our-- of our childhood is that it was a very happy one. Now, because my father was working in the factory, my mother thought it would be wise to start a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she obtained soaps, because it is a so-- it's a-- a-- a coal district there. And she thought soaps would be a good idea to make a living from. So she obtained the soaps. And because we didn't have any finances, she arranged a push cart. And she went from village to village to sell these soaps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it flourished. And my father soon help-- came out. And he also started a wholesale business in perfumes and in soaps. With the time-- like it was now, let's say, '23, '24-- in time, they made a little money, and they bought a plot in Berrenrath, which is about another two or three kilometers away from Knapsack. I'm not sure about these kilometers, but more or less. Berrenrath was a large village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Berrenrath for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-R-E-N-R-A-T-H, Berrenrath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, they bought a plot and they built themselves a-- a-- a house where-- in the-- at the [FRENCH], where they had a business. And we lived above. That was done in Germany. I suppose it's done here too. And then the business flourished, and everything went fine. I remember only good things, warmth in the-- in the family. We were the only Jews in Berrenrath, and we were respected. There was one-- one discord in my childhood at that time. And that was that the children were playing together, and I was one of the children. And I must have been five or six years or something like that. We were playing in the yard downstairs, hop scotch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when one little girl turned around to the other one and said, let's not play with Santa. And the other little girl asked, why not? Oh, she killed Christ. So I didn't know what she meant. I had never heard about this. Yes. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I cried because they didn't want to play with me, and I was now ostracized because I killed Christ. And I went to my mother, and I said, I didn't kill anyone. I didn't kill anyone. Why do they say-- why do they accuse me? And my mother, of course, understood very well, having lived in Poland and having had the persecutions. The-- the Poles were, you know, they were always very anti-Semitic and persecuting the Jews. She took me around and she consoled me. And she said, of course, you didn't Chr-- kill anyone. And that was the first instance that I felt that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm different. But until now-- then, I didn't know any difference. And another thing is as a child, it doesn't matter how old you are. From the age of three, four, five, you become part of the country you-- you live in.

speaker: interviewer
text: My children were Rhodesian children. They were very English orientated. So I was German orientated. And I was going for the-- I was a German Jew, you see? Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even so much think of this being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was German. I was there. And that-- that incident, of course, put the first thought in my eyes. I'm different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I started school. And it was one winter, and I must have been seven years old because I was young. It was most probably the second year at school. And when I start-- in winter, the-- the children were-- it was still very hard for the Germans, you see. The-- the war devastated Germany. Well, the loss of the war devastated Germany, and they had to pay so much reparation to the Allies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans were-- were poor. There was just not enough. So there were many poor children as well. And the-- the school organized that hot milk should be distributed to the children during the interval. And they had to bring a mug. I brought my mug like everybody else. And we had to stand in-- in queues. And as I came nearer, the teacher-- wasn't my teacher, but she was a teacher of the school-- she waved me aside. You don't need any. She didn't say why. She didn't say anything. But I didn't need any. Everybody else ne-- got it. That was another knock. And it made me already apprehensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it? What is it? Why? I've done nothing wrong. I'm a-- I'm a prop-- I'm a good pupil. Another thing is it was a Catholic school. People are-- the majority are Catholics. A lot of Protestants, of course, because of Luther, yeah, who came about in the 15th century.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was a Catholic school. And in the morning, we arrived at school at 8 o'clock. And we all went into church. I went with, because school girls, I-- I went with. And at the-- at the entrance, you dipped your hand into the water and you made a crucifix-- the-- the cross, the sign of the cross. I did that too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went inside, but I didn't have to pray. So I looked around at all the nice figures. I said, it's very pretty, very nice. But I didn't have to pray. That I didn't do. And then one day, I told my parents about it. Well, I didn't have to go to church anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That was my childhood. Everything went fine. And my parents worked themselves up. And-- and they had a good name. People loved them. They liked them because they were good people. They did not think to take out German citizenship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? I still cannot fathom until today. But they did not. So they were Polish citizens. They didn't use their passports. They didn't travel. They had forgot about it, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we all were born in Germany, we children. Usually, when a child is born in a country, the child gets the citizenship of the country. Not so in Germany. I still haven't got a German passport. I worked for it. I asked for it while I was in Rhodesia. No, they wouldn't do it. Your father-- if your father was born in Germany, then you could have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 19-- until 1928, everything went fine. Everything was-- it-- it was peaceful for everybody. Germans were wonderful. Then in 1928, we hear-- heard rumors of Munich. In Munich, there was a putsch. There was an uproar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody called Hitler was put into pri-- Hitler and his another helpers, or whatever you call them, he was put into prison. Well, it didn't have a great effect. But already, something started. They don't want the Jews. We heard already saying [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The Jews are our misfortunes. It didn't disturb us. We were in Berrenrath, far away from everything. But slowly, slowly, it infiltrated. You know, people started talking already. Even the Germans who were nice to us were already cal-- looking funny at us. Well, 19-- 1928, '29, '20-- and I must say that in 1928, my father decided-- or my parents decided-- that schooling, we haven't got enough Jewish influence. Being Jewish, and especially coming from Poland, you know how very Jewish the people were there-- you don't-- you can think of how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And being Jewish, they felt we should have more Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Education, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: --education. So I was inscribed into a-- a high school. It was called the Lyceum. The Lyceum Yavne. Yavne, it was a town-- was a town during the uprising of Bar Kokhba, of course, in Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the Yavne, the high school. And my brothers went to the Moriah. They were junior school. Every day, we had to travel a long, long way. And that was very, very hazardous. We had to get up 5 o'clock in the morning to catch the train. I had to look after these two wild brothers of mine, who pulled my pi-- my pigtails. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it wasn't a pleasure. Half an hour by train, and then still the trams, and-- and herding these two boys when everybody wanted to get to work. And it was-- it wasn't easy. I didn't have a good time. I didn't like my school years. But I learned, of course, because you go to school, you learn whether you like it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that-- so the time went. And, of course, Nazism reared its heard more and more. And we became very ap-- apprehensive about this. What happened then in 1933, Wahlen. Wahlen means-- what? [LAUGHS] What, an uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, elections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, elections.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elections. And Hitler came in with the help of-- of Hindenburg, the President. With his help. It was all connived, of course. And they came in and immediately felt-- you felt a difference. In Berrenrath, also, it was arranged for youth. You-- you mustn't forget there were-- there was a lot of misery in Germany. And the youth were in the streets like we have them here in South Africa now, no work, vandalism, and things like this, because they-- they had nothing to live for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no moral-- morals anymore. So the-- what the Nazi machine did very, very cleverly, they took them all in and put them into uniform. As soon as you put somebody into uniform, he becomes a-- a somebody. Also, they were-- that-- that, I know now. We didn't know that at that time. But they were being indoc-- indoctrinated at-- in the camps. They were told how great Germany is and how wonderful it is. It's true they were beaten, but they will not be able to be pushed low again. And how we must fight for our fatherland. 'Deutschland Deutschland Uber Alles,' and all this. And they were told another thing. If you hear at home that your parents are talking against the party, you must come and report it. And many a German father or mother or both were taken into prison. The hunt for the communists, because communism had really come up very strongly because of-- because of the misery, you see, come very strongly. And also because of the example in-- in Russia. And the news went, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was something new. Maybe this would help Germany, you see? So a great proportion of Germans were communists. And the Nazism worked against that. So the-- the children were also asked to report on whose parents are communists. And many German parents, people, were imprisoned because of that. And then, of course, we started being very frightened. At night, the youngsters were told to go with-- what did the Romans have, flares? What do you call this? Flares.

speaker: interviewee
text: Flares. They would go with these flares at night-- and in Berrenrath, there were no lights in the street at that time-- and sing their German songs, the Horst Wessell song, which became their anthem. 'Deutschland, Deutschland,' of course. 'Deutschland, Deutschland Uber Alles' was their anthem. But this became the Nazi song, Horst Wessell song, because I believe he was a martyr early in the-- in the-- in their struggle. And when they passed our house, we closed the shutters. We looked through the shutters. And it was eerie. It was frightening. The whole thing had changed. We couldn't understand what had happened. And then in June-- in June, all of a sudden, very late at night-- it must have been like 11 o'clock at night-- there was a knock on the door. And you get, of course, very frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother opened the door, and there was a lady standing there, who was a customer of hers. She was-- she was the wife of a official from the-- from the-- from the factory there. He was a great official. And she came to tell-- warn my mother. She said, my husband has just returned from a meeting in town, in-- in this village. And he said the meeting was very rowdy. And somebody got up and says, there is a Jew living in this town. And this Jew has-- is sucking our blood. Look, he has got two houses. One house is not enough for him. They're sucking our blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: They forgot to think that first of all, they worked very, very hard, both of them, my father and my mother. And secondly, they were frugal. They were careful with what they did. They invested what-- what-- their fruits of their labors. The invested in another house, thinking it will help them, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, that wasn't good for the Nazis because, of course, the Jews are sucking our blood. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The Jews are our misfortunes. That became a slogan. That was all over, in the cinemas and all over. And we were-- became very frightened. When this lady now came and said that this-- it was decided that this Jew must be imprisoned. She said, tell your husband to take-- we didn't have a car, somehow. We had bicycles. Take his-- take his-- go immediately to the-- to Holland. And my-- of course, we trembled. I heard it. I was there with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: We trembled. And the woman says, please don't give me away. Oh, she was frightened. She understood. And she-- she urged my father to go. And he says, how can I leave my family? Whatever happens, I stay with my family. And he stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I remember the-- the-- the date 16. Whether it was Friday night, I still-- I don't know. But I know it was Friday night that-- that somebody came to say, there is a rumor or rumors that the policeman will come and-- and arrest your father. But that policeman was ordered to arrest my father. And he got himself drunk because he loved my father. He liked my father very much. He didn't want to-- to arrest my father. So he got himself very drunk. But it didn't help much, because two-- I don't know whether they were Gestapo, but they were in their-- not in uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came in their trench coat, you know? This-- this-- like a rain coat. They ca-- took this drunk policeman. Apparently, in those days, they were not allowed yet to arrest just anyhow they liked later on, you see. But they had to have the policeman. It was order still. Still order. So I remember it was Friday night. Like today, I remembered a knock on the door. And my mother had just lit the candles, the Friday night candles. And of course, we were very apprehensive for-- for-- from the rumors. And I don't remember who opened the door. We had a maid. May-- maybe she opened the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: But up came this policeman, drunk like anything, and say-- Erder, I have to arrest you. And these two men were just standing quietly there. And my father turned around-- because we knew already-- my father turned around. I will come if you let me finish my ceremony. And he made the Kiddush and the Motzi. He did not eat. And he went. The next day, my mother went-- they had took him to Mulheim. That is further than Knapsack, more-- more in the middle, between Cologne and Berrenrath. To Mulheim. It was a bigger-- it was like a small town. And they had police stations there, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can see the cord.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, my mother visited him, and she found some [INAUDIBLE] cowered in a corner. And he was frightened even to talk. He was trembling. And then eventually, she told-- he told her that there was so much screaming and shouting during the night, and sc-- people being pulled out of their cells and schlepped along into the corridor and shot. He heard shots after shots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they came into his cell and they beat him up black and blue. And as he cowered in the corner, the whole side here was black and blue. Well, how do I-- I saw it, actually, because a few days later, I went to bring him some food. And I saw it. And of course, I cried. He cried. But what we-- could we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a week-- yeah, the-- the people who were-- were pulled out screaming, they were communists. The Nazis wanted to erad-- eradicate communism. That was just the opposite of what they wanted. They wanted the nationalism, you see. So the communists were el-- tri-- they tried to eliminate them. Also, the rumors, all rumors, that people were found shot in the gutters, str-- also pulled out from the homes, and just got-- not Jews, communists. All communists. Eradicate the communists because they are the enemies. And then, of course, it was the Juden. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. After a week, my father was transferred to a penitentiary. A penitentiary is, of course, for murderers. But-- but I don't know if they had-- didn't have any more murderers. But these sort of people, like my father and so on, who were the enemies of the country. They were taken to-- they were taken to this penitentiary. Now, of course, we were agitated, terribly agitated. My mother tried now to get him out, but nothing doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had two brothers. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the penitentiary?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cologne, itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cologne.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Koln. In Koln itself. And so my-- my mother contacted his two brothers. They, of course, were also very worried about it. She contacted his two brothers. And one brother couldn't do a thing. But the other brother, the eldest-- my father was the middle one-- the eldest brother-- yeah, they also had come to Germany, these two brothers, because my father brought them. He says, look, there's peace here. There's no-- no persecution for-- after the Jews. There's no persecution. So they were there. And they made a living. Nothing great, but they made a living. They were happy with their families. The eldest brother in his youth had been a vaudeville artist. That's where Aviva gets it from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was on the stage. But that, of course-- he was a black sheep. You don't do this in a-- in a respectable family. And they didn't like it. The family didn't like it. So he, being a vaudeville artist, he was on stage. He was with other artists. And there was this German, young German man like him, and they became friends, very close friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when this happened with my father, he thought of-- of this German friend. Maybe he can help. When he visited him to talk to him, he saw an SS uniform with the insignia of the-- of the-- the skeleton on top. He told him his plight. He says, I'll try. And he got my father out after a month. Now, if you stayed-- if you were kept there, after a month you were being transferred to Dachau. Because they had-- made-- make place for new ones, you see? So Dachau was at that time the camp where these people were being sent after a month. Now, it wasn't yet a month. That's why he was able-- this SS officer was able to get my father out with the proviso to leave within one week. Of course, he could have gone to Poland because he had a Polish passport, you see. But who wants to go to Poland? Poland, where you are being persecuted? No, he wants-- now-- they work, now, you see. My mother, my father. My father was very disturbed when he came out. He wasn't the same person.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was before a strapping man. You saw the picture. I showed you the photo. He was good-looking. Here, he came out haggard, disheveled, nervous. He didn't want anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said, but we have got to do something. So she ran to the consulates. Denmark? No. Afterwards, they did help. Denmark, no. France. Oh, you can't get into France because they are overrun with refugees. People had to run now. You see, communists and Jews had run.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even Holland had closed the border at that time. Later on, they opened it. But at that time-- people got in by hook and by crook, you know, but not direct. My parents didn't know about this. They were direct people. They were honest people. They had to do everything the way it should have-- it should be done. So-- and also, my father wasn't in the-- in the condition to try to get in through the back door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eventually, do you know who gave the passport-- [GERMAN] --that he could stay for good? Spain. Spain, who had expelled the Jews in the 15th century. In 1492, all Jews had to leave because the Catholic country didn't want any Jews. Either convert or leave. And the Jews at that time left. And of course, you know, they-- from a great, great power, they went-- became nothing. They really became nothing. Why? Because they didn't-- they didn't want the Jews. Serves them right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, anyhow, the thing is that Spain granted him a visa, full visa to go and live there. Now, because my father was so nervous, my mother thought, she can't let him go like that. So she sent my brother, Leon. We didn't-- at that time, he wasn't called Leon yet. And we called him afterwards Leon. I'll tell you just now why. He was Leib. But he was also Levi for school. So she sent my brother, Leib Levi, with my father to look after this man. He was 12 years old. So they had to leave. Within a week, he had to leave. So they left. They left, and we were left then. My mother tried now at her best. Oh, yes. He was given 1,000 marks. That, he was allowed to take out. 1,000 marks. I don't know what the value was at that time, whether it was a lot. I only know that when eventually he did come to Spain, he bought an old dilapidated farmhouse, which wasn't really a farmhouse. It was like a hut with three rooms. The toilet's outside. Really dilapidated. And that is what he bought for the 1,000 marks, or whatever it cost at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was left in Germany, and I carried on going to school. And so did Chaim, my other-- the youngest brother. And she managed to sell the business eventually. And I remember when we sold it, and it was the-- her work. She worked so hard all the time. And as they con-- con-- concluded the deal, we sat on the steps which led up to our living rooms, and we cried bitterly. What has happened? What has happened to people who have done no wrong to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she then applied to-- she then also got her visa to go to-- to Spain with my younger brother. I was to remain to finish my schooling. I couldn't do much. My mind wasn't there, really. But she left. She was also allowed to take with her 1,000 marks. And off she went to-- to meet my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I was left in Germany with an uncle and aunt, one of the brothers of my father. And there, I didn't have a good time. I wasn't liked very much. I don't know why. Maybe I was too morose or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I got a letter from my father to say that your schooling is not enough for here. It is very important that you should have a trade. Go and learn hairdressing. So I went to a hairdresser and I paid, so I could learn hairdressing. And I learned the marcel, you know, how they did that with a hot iron. I learned that. And my mother had left in November, 1933. And a year later in November, 1934, I left, and I came to-- to Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you stop it there?
